name: Adi Sankara
dates_notes: Traditional dates from sectarian chronicles place him 788–820; academic consensus (associated with Hacker and Mayeda) is somewhat earlier. Hacker's monograph and Mayeda's *A Thousand Teachings* are not on disk in our corpus; cited as field-orientation. The securely-authored corpus is much smaller than the traditional ascription set.

lineage_polemical.note: Bhāskara explicitly attacks Śaṅkara's māyā as a smuggled-in Buddhist concept; the polemic runs both directions in the commentarial tradition.

Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya 2.2.37 develops Bādarāyaṇa's anti-Pāśupata argument: the pati-paśu-pāśa account either makes Śiva incompetent (if external pāśa-bonds limit him) or makes Śiva cruel (if he himself binds the paśu); the argument addresses the Lakulīśa-Kauṇḍinya position.
Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya 2.2.37 develops the anti-Pāśupata argument with Kauṇḍinya's Pañcārtha-Bhāṣya systematisation in view.
Śaṅkara's Bṛhadāraṇyaka-Bhāṣya is the principal vehicle for the refutation of Bhartṛprapañca's bhedābheda.

Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya 2.2.11–17 develops Bādarāyaṇa's refutation of Vaiśeṣika atomism: paramāṇus, being acetana, cannot self-initiate conjunction; a conscious cause is required, contrary to Vaiśeṣika atheism.

Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya 2.2.11–17 engages the developed Vaiśeṣika position represented by Praśastapāda's Padārtha-Dharma-Saṅgraha — the classical theistic-cosmological articulation — not merely Kaṇāda's bare sūtras.

Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya 2.2.33–36 develops Bādarāyaṇa's anti-Jaina argument: anekānta-vāda either collapses into self-contradiction or trivializes into the unreal thesis that everything is somehow both A and not-A from different standpoints.

Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya 2.2.33–36's anti-Jaina argument applies with particular force to Kundakunda's position, which makes the niścaya / vyavahāra distinction structurally load-bearing — exactly the kind of two-tier framework Śaṅkara's vyāvahārika / pāramārthika scheme parallels and contests.

Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya 3.3.53 develops the anti-Lokāyata argument: consciousness cannot be reduced to a property of bodily elements because no material aggregate possesses cetanā in its constituent parts, and the supposed emergence of consciousness from acetana materials is unaccounted-for.

Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya 1.1.4 engages the Mīmāṃsā position on śruti's tātparya as found in Śabara — that all of śruti is ultimately codanā-pravartaka — and refutes it with the doctrine of the brahma-paratva of the upaniṣadic mahā-vākyas.

Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya 2.2.32 (vaināśika-pakṣasya nirūpaṇam) treats the śūnyavādin position with curt dismissal — Śaṅkara writes that no Vedāntic position rests on a foundation similar enough to require argumentative engagement. The brevity of the dismissal is itself doctrinally significant: the structural homology between Mādhyamaka two-truths and the Advaitic vyāvahārika / pāramārthika scheme is what later Bhedābhedins will exploit as the pracchanna-bauddha charge.

Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya 2.2.28–32 develops the Vedāntic refutation of vijñāna-vāda specifically: the bāhyārtha-vāda objection presses that the very arising of the cognition 'this is a pillar' presupposes a referent extra-cognitive enough to ground the cognition's content; the Yogācāra dream-analogy fails because dreams are themselves explained by reference to waking-perception of external objects.

The BSB 2.2.32 dismissal of the śūnyavādin position applies in particular to the Prāsaṅgika construal: the refusal even of conventional posit makes the position, on Śaṅkara's reading, even less compatible with the Vedāntic affirmation of brahman as the satya-jagat-kāraṇa than the Sākāra Yogācāra positions are.

Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya 2.2.18–20 develops the Vedāntic refutation of the Sarvāstivāda-Vaibhāṣika position whose principal exposition is Vasubandhu's Abhidharma-Kośa-Bhāṣya: the kṣaṇika-vāda cannot account for recognition (pratyabhijñā) across moments without smuggling in the very kind of persisting substrate it denies; the doctrine of momentary dharmas with svabhāva therefore collapses under the same considerations Vasubandhu's own Sautrāntika gloss had already begun to press.

core_thesis: Śaṅkara argues, in *Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya* 1.1.1 and across the four *adhyāyas* of that commentary, that *brahman* alone is real. *Brahman* is *sat-cit-ānanda*, partless, and in its highest description *nirguṇa* (Taittirīya-Bhāṣya 2.1.1, on *satyaṃ jñānam anantaṃ brahma* read as *svarūpa-lakṣaṇa*) — not 'attributeless' in any bare-logical sense (*brahman* remains *cit-svarūpa* and *ānanda-svarūpa*) but *guṇātīta*, beyond the three *guṇa*s of *prakṛti* (*sattva*, *rajas*, *tamas*), and *nirupādhika*, free of the *upādhi*-borne predicates that constitute the *jīva*-*Īśvara*-*jagat* differentiations (Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya 1.1.11; 3.2.11–21). The *jīva* is non-different from *brahman* in essence — the position is fixed by the *mahāvākya tat tvam asi*, on which Śaṅkara's *Chāndogya-Upaniṣad-Bhāṣya* 6.8.7–6.16.3 is the *locus classicus*. The phenomenal world is *mithyā*: not *asat* (sheer non-existent), and not *sat* in the way *brahman* is, but *anirvacanīya* — indeterminable on the binary — and sublatable in *brahma-jñāna* (Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya 2.1.14, on *tad-ananyatva*). The mechanism of apparent multiplicity is *adhyāsa* (superimposition). The *adhyāsa-bhāṣya* prefacing BSB 1.1.1 analyzes this: the *anātman* is mistakenly superimposed on the *ātman* and conversely, generating the entire transactional field of *pramātṛ*-*pramāṇa*-*prameya*. Cosmologically Śaṅkara defends *vivarta-vāda* (apparent transformation) against the *pariṇāma-vāda* of Sāṅkhya and Bhāskara — most fully at *Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya* 2.1.14–20 and 2.1.26–27. Liberating cognition is *akhaṇḍākāra-vṛtti*, arising from *mahāvākya*-instruction (Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya 1.1.4, *tat tu samanvayāt*). *Karma* is preparatory and cannot of itself produce *mokṣa*, because *karma* presupposes the agent–object division that *jñāna* dissolves (Bhagavad-Gītā-Bhāṣya, *upodghāta*; Īśa-Bhāṣya 9–11). The *jīvanmukta* lives out the residue of *prārabdha-karma* in a body whose phenomenality has ceased to be cognitively binding (Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya 4.1.15, 4.1.19).

engaged_works.title: Brahma-Sutra-Bhashya
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored; the school-defining commentary on the Brahma-Sūtra.

ascription_evidence.description: Universal modern consensus on Śaṅkara's authorship.

engaged_works.summary: Śaṅkara's school-defining *bhāṣya* on Bādarāyaṇa's *Brahma-Sūtra*, organized by the four *adhyāyas* and their *pādas*. The work opens with the *adhyāsa-bhāṣya* — Śaṅkara's prefatory analysis of *adhyāsa* (superimposition) as the universal pre-philosophical condition that makes ordinary cognition, and Vedic injunction itself, possible. The doctrinal hinges fall in successive loci. BSB 1.1.4 (*tat tu samanvayāt*) defends the thesis that *brahman* is the *tātparya* (settled purport) of all Upaniṣadic statements, against the Mīmāṃsaka thesis of *karma-pradhānya*. BSB 2.1 refutes the Sāṅkhya *pradhāna* and the *pariṇāma-vāda* alternative, and defends *vivarta*. BSB 2.2 refutes, in turn, Vaiśeṣika atomism, the Buddhist schools (Vijñānavāda, Sautrāntika, Vaibhāṣika, Mādhyamika), Jaina *anekānta*, Pāśupata, and Pāñcarātra. BSB 3.2 analyzes the *avasthās* and the *jīva*'s identity with *brahman*. BSB 4 treats *kramamukti*, *jīvanmukti*, and the trajectory of the *mukta-puruṣa*. Securely authored; the cornerstone of every later Advaita sub-school.

engaged_works.title: Bhagavad-Gita-Bhashya
engaged_works.composition_dates_notes: Composed in Śaṅkara's mature period; relative chronology with the Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya disputed but the two are clearly companion works.
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored; the school-defining Gītā commentary and one of the four uncontested *bhāṣyas* of Śaṅkara.

ascription_evidence.description: Universal modern consensus; Hacker, Mayeda, and Halbfass all treat it as authentic (none of these scholars' English studies is on disk in our corpus; cited as field-standard summary).

engaged_works.summary: Śaṅkara's prose *bhāṣya* on the *Bhagavad-Gītā*. The work establishes the *Gītā* as a *prasthāna* on equal footing with *Upaniṣad* and *Brahma-Sūtra*. It opens with an *upodghāta* arguing that the *Gītā*'s *tātparya* is *jñāna*-mediated *mokṣa*, and that *karma* and *jñāna* belong to mutually exclusive *adhikāra*-orders. *Karma* is preparatory; it is never co-instrumental in the rise of liberating cognition. The principal interpretive moves follow the chapter sequence. Chapter 2 is treated as the analytic exposition of *sāṃkhya* and *yoga*. The apparent *karma*-injunction at 3.8 (*niyataṃ kuru karma*) is read as addressed to the still-unprepared seeker, not as a counter-claim against the *jñāna-niṣṭhā* doctrine. Chapter 13 reads *kṣetra-kṣetrajña* as the *adhyāsa*-structure of cognition. Chapter 15 identifies the *puruṣottama* with *nirguṇa brahman* under devotional aspect. The *bhāṣya* fixes the *jñāna-niṣṭhā* / *karma-niṣṭhā* polarity that every later Vedāntin — Bhāskara, Rāmānuja, Madhva — must accept or contest. Universally accepted as authentic in the academic ascription set; Hacker, Mayeda, and Halbfass treat it as such (none of these scholars' English studies is on disk in our corpus).

engaged_works.title: Chandogya-Upanisad-Bhashya
engaged_works.composition_dates_notes: Companion to the Bṛhadāraṇyaka-Bhāṣya in the mature Upaniṣad-bhāṣya cycle.
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored; one of the ten principal Upaniṣad-bhāṣyas universally accepted as Śaṅkara's.
engaged_works.summary: Commentary on the Chāndogya-Upaniṣad — the longest of the Sāmaveda Upaniṣads and the second-largest principal Upaniṣad after the Bṛhadāraṇyaka. Organized by *prapāṭhaka* and *khaṇḍa*. The *bhāṣya* treats the great pedagogical sequences of the text in turn: the *Sad-Vidyā* of Uddālaka and Śvetaketu (chapter 6), with the *mahāvākya tat tvam asi* repeated nine times as the climactic instruction; the *Bhūma-Vidyā* of Sanatkumāra and Nārada (chapter 7); the *Dahara-Vidyā* of the heart-space (chapter 8); the *Sāma-vidyās* of the early chapters. Śaṅkara's analysis of *tat tvam asi* — and in particular his use of *jahad-ajahal-lakṣaṇā* (joint inclusive-exclusive implication, by which the apparently contradictory predication of *brahman* on *jīva* is resolved through partial retention of meaning) — is the school's *locus classicus* for identity-instruction. The *bhāṣya* also stages a sustained refutation of *bhedābheda* readings of the *Sad-Vidyā* and rejects any *upādhi*-mediated reading that would soften the *aikya*-thesis. Sureśvara composed no separate *vārttika* on the Chāndogya; the *bhāṣya* itself is the school's definitive treatment.

engaged_works.title: Aitareya-Upanisad-Bhashya
engaged_works.composition_dates_notes: Part of the standard ten-Upaniṣad cycle.
engaged_works.summary: Short commentary on the Aitareya-Upaniṣad of the Ṛgveda. The Upaniṣad runs to three *adhyāyas*: the cosmogonic opening (*ātman*'s creation of the *lokas* and the *loka-pālas*); the analysis of the threefold birth of the *jīva* (gestational, post-natal, and eschatological re-birth into the next world); and the climactic identification *prajñānam brahma* — one of the four canonical *mahāvākyas*. Śaṅkara reads the cosmogony as *arthavāda* attached to the doctrinal core, not as a competing positive ontology. He treats the threefold-birth analysis as preparatory disidentification of the *jīva* from gross corporeal embodiment. On the *prajñānam brahma* equation he gives his standard reading: *prajñāna* (consciousness) is *brahman* not as a *viśeṣaṇa* (qualifying attribute) but as *svarūpa-lakṣaṇa* (definition by essential nature). The reading is continuous with his treatment of *satyaṃ jñānam anantaṃ brahma* in the Taittirīya. Brief but doctrinally pivotal: this *bhāṣya* establishes the consciousness-identification reading of the *mahāvākya* against any *prajñā*-as-*guṇa* alternative.

engaged_works.title: Prashna-Upanisad-Bhashya
engaged_works.composition_dates_notes: Part of the standard ten-Upaniṣad cycle; companion to the Muṇḍaka-Bhāṣya in the Atharvavedic group.
engaged_works.summary: Commentary on the Praśna-Upaniṣad, the Atharvavedic Upaniṣad organized as six *praśnas* (questions) put by six disciples to the sage Pippalāda. Each question takes up one topic of *upaniṣadic vidyā*. *Praśna* 1: the origin of *prajā*. *Praśna* 2: the relative ranking of *prāṇa* and the senses. *Praśna* 3: the genesis and operation of *prāṇa*. *Praśna* 4: the four states of consciousness — *jāgrat*, *svapna*, *suṣupti*, and the implicit fourth. *Praśna* 5: the meditative power of the *praṇava* (*oṃkāra*). *Praśna* 6: the *ṣoḍaśa-kala-puruṣa*, the sixteen-fold *puruṣa* whose *kalās* dissolve in *brahman*-knowledge. Śaṅkara's *bhāṣya* reads the text as a graded pedagogical ascent from cosmology to *praṇava*-meditation to terminal *puruṣa-vidyā*. His analysis of the four states in *Praśna* 4 prefigures the fuller treatment in the *Māṇḍūkya-Bhāṣya*; his analysis of the *ṣoḍaśa-kala* doctrine becomes the *locus classicus* for the dissolution of *upādhis* in *brahman*-cognition.

engaged_works.title: Mundaka-Upanisad-Bhashya
engaged_works.composition_dates_notes: Part of the standard ten-Upaniṣad cycle.
engaged_works.summary: Commentary on the Muṇḍaka-Upaniṣad of the Atharvaveda, in three *muṇḍakas* of two *khaṇḍas* each. The Upaniṣad opens with the canonical *parā-vidyā* / *aparā-vidyā* distinction (Muṇḍaka 1.1.4–5): the higher knowledge by which the imperishable is known; the lower, comprising the four Vedas with their auxiliaries. Śaṅkara takes this distinction as decisive for the *jñāna* / *karma* hierarchy. The second *muṇḍaka* contains the *dvā suparṇā* verse (Muṇḍaka 3.1.1; parallel at Ṛg-veda 1.164.20) of the two birds on one tree. Śaṅkara reads it as the *jīva*-*Īśvara* relation under *adhyāsa*: the eating bird is the apparently-distinct *jīva*, the watching bird is *brahman* as *sākṣin*, and *mokṣa* is the recognition that the eating bird is in truth the watching bird. The *bhāṣya* also fixes the Advaita reading of *paramaṃ sāmyam* (Muṇḍaka 3.1.3) as identity rather than mere likeness — a reading the *Vaiṣṇava* commentators (Rāmānuja, Madhva) will resist. The text's compact verses make it pedagogically central in the Advaita curriculum.

engaged_works.title: Katha-Upanisad-Bhashya
engaged_works.composition_dates_notes: Part of the standard ten-Upaniṣad cycle.
engaged_works.summary: Commentary on the Kaṭha-Upaniṣad of the Kṛṣṇa-Yajurveda — the dialogue of Naciketas and Yama in two *adhyāyas* of three *vallīs* each. Śaṅkara reads the text as graded instruction. Yama's three boons stage the seeker's progressive renunciation of householder goods, heavenly goods, and finally even the longing for self-knowledge as a possession. The central *vallīs* unfold the *ātma-vidyā* through the *rathakalpana* (chariot-allegory: *ātman* as rider, *buddhi* as charioteer, *manas* as reins, *indriyas* as horses, *viṣayas* as paths) and through the analysis of the *aṅguṣṭha-mātra puruṣa*. The *bhāṣya* fixes the Advaita reading of *nāyam ātmā pravacanena labhyaḥ* (Kaṭha 1.2.23): the *ātman* is not attained by mere instruction or inference, but *svayaṃ vivṛṇute tanūṃ svām* — "reveals its own form by itself." This is the school's *locus* for the role of self-disclosure (and, in later commentators, of grace) in the rise of *aparokṣa-jñāna*. Equally consequential is the treatment of the *ātman*'s subtle quantification ("thumb-sized") as figurative rather than ontological.

engaged_works.title: Mandukya-Upanisad-Bhashya with Gaudapada-Karika-Bhashya
engaged_works.composition_dates_notes: Composed as a unified commentary on the twelve mantras of the Upaniṣad together with all four prakaraṇas of Gauḍapāda's Kārikā.
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored; the unified commentary on the Upaniṣad and Gauḍapāda's Kārikā is the school's principal source on the four states of consciousness and on *ajāti-vāda*.
engaged_works.summary: Unified commentary on the twelve mantras of the Māṇḍūkya-Upaniṣad together with the four *prakaraṇas* of Gauḍapāda's *Māṇḍūkya-Kārikā* (*Āgama*, *Vaitathya*, *Advaita*, *Alātaśānti*). Śaṅkara treats the Upaniṣad's analysis of the four *pādas* of *Oṃ* — corresponding to *jāgrat*, *svapna*, *suṣupti*, and *turīya* — as the canonical structural map of consciousness. *Turīya* is the non-relational *ātman* whose recognition is *mokṣa*. On the Kārikā he gives his most extended exposition of *ajāti-vāda* (the doctrine that nothing has ever truly come into being), of the *vaitathya* (unreality) of waking and dream as parallel *vyāvahārika* orders, and of the *alāta-cakra* (firebrand-circle) analogy by which apparent multiplicity is shown to be the trace of an unmoving consciousness. Modern scholarship has questioned the unity of the four *prakaraṇas* — Hacker's stylometric work and King's *Early Advaita Vedānta and Buddhism* (1995) raise the strongest doubts about *prakaraṇa* 4 — but Śaṅkara reads them as a single integrated treatise. The work is doctrinally pivotal for the analysis of *mithyā* as neither *sat* nor *asat* but as cognitively sublatable.

engaged_works.title: Kena-Upanisad-Bhashya (pada-bhashya and vakya-bhashya)
engaged_works.composition_dates_notes: Two distinct bhāṣyas on the same Upaniṣad; both transmitted under Śaṅkara's name.
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Both *pada-bhāṣya* (word-by-word) and *vākya-bhāṣya* (sentence-by-sentence) are securely ascribed to Śaṅkara; the existence of two parallel commentaries on a single short Upaniṣad is itself unusual and characteristic of the text's importance in the school.
engaged_works.summary: Two distinct *bhāṣyas* on the Kena-Upaniṣad of the Sāmaveda. The *pada-bhāṣya* gives a word-by-word gloss; the *vākya-bhāṣya* gives a sentence-by-sentence and topically-organized exposition. The existence of two parallel commentaries by the same author on a single short Upaniṣad is itself unusual and signals the text's importance. The Upaniṣad opens with the question *keneṣitaṃ patati preṣitaṃ manaḥ* — "by what is the mind impelled to its target?" — driving toward the recognition that the mover behind every cognitive faculty is *brahman* itself, knowable not as an object but only as the *pratyag-ātman* of every act of cognition. Śaṅkara's signature reading falls on Kena 1.5, *yan manasā na manute yenāhur mano matam*: *brahman* is *avijñātaṃ vijānatām* (unknown to those who think they know it) and *vijñātam avijānatām* (known to those who know themselves not to know it). This is the apophatic *locus classicus* for the school. The second half of the *Yakṣa* allegory — the failure of Agni, Vāyu, and Indra to comprehend the *Yakṣa*-*Brahman* — is read as figurative dramatization of the same point: *brahman* is the ground of agency that no agent can objectify.

engaged_works.title: Isha-Upanisad-Bhashya
engaged_works.composition_dates_notes: Part of the standard ten-Upaniṣad cycle.
engaged_works.summary: Commentary on the Īśa-Upaniṣad — the shortest of the principal Upaniṣads, eighteen mantras long, embedded within the Vājasaneyi-Saṃhitā of the Śukla-Yajurveda (hence its alternative title *Vājasaneyi-Saṃhitā-Upaniṣad*). The doctrinally pivotal section is mantras 9–11, where the text appears to prescribe *karma* alongside *vidyā*: *vidyāṃ ca avidyāṃ ca yas tad vedobhayaṃ saha*. Śaṅkara refuses the *jñāna-karma-samuccaya* construal that Bhartṛprapañca and others draw from this passage. He reads the *avidyā* / *vidyā* pair as *karma* and *upāsanā* — both preparatory disciplines belonging to the householder *adhikārin*, neither itself constituting *brahma-vidyā*. The Īśa-Bhāṣya is therefore the single most important short text for the school's *jñāna-karma-vibhāga* doctrine; Śaṅkara's specific reading of *vidyā* in these mantras becomes the standard reference in every later debate on the question. Mantras 4–8 give the paradoxical descriptions of the *ātman* (*tad ejati tad naijati*: "It moves, it does not move"); Śaṅkara reads these as *adhyāsa*-talk rather than as positive ontology.

engaged_works.title: Upadesha-Sahasri
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored; Mayeda's critical edition establishes a stable text.
engaged_works.summary: Śaṅkara's only securely authored independent (non-commentarial) *prakaraṇa*. It is in two parts. The *gadya* (prose) section runs to three chapters and is often treated as the doctrinal core. The *padya* (metrical) section runs to nineteen chapters and develops particular topics in verse. The work is pedagogically structured: it presents the teacher–pupil dialogue as the *sāmpradāyika* (lineage-transmitted) means of liberating instruction. The central topics are the analysis of *aham*-cognition, the *dṛk*–*dṛśya-viveka* (discrimination of the seer from the seen — consciousness as *sākṣin* against its objects), the *adhyāsa* of *anātman* on *ātman*, the dismantling of the attribution of *kartṛtva* and *bhoktṛtva* to the *ātman*, and the operation of *mahāvākya*-instruction. The most direct statement of the *jñāna*-only doctrine is at *Padya* I.11.15 (*na tato 'mṛtatāśāsti, karmaṇo 'jñāna-hetutaḥ*): action cannot yield immortality, because action arises from ignorance, and *mokṣa* depends on *jñāna* alone. Mayeda's 1973 critical edition (Tokyo; reissued Motilal Banarsidass 2006) is the standard text and underlies the GRETIL Upadeśa-Sāhasrī e-text on disk; Mayeda's English translation and analytic apparatus is widely cited as the basis of modern academic engagement (the English study itself is not on disk in our corpus).

engaged_works.title: Brhadaranyaka-Bhashya
engaged_works.summary: Śaṅkara's longest Upaniṣad-commentary, on the Bṛhadāraṇyaka — itself the largest of the principal Upaniṣads. Organized by *adhyāya* and *brāhmaṇa*. The *bhāṣya* treats the great Yājñavalkya dialogues (with Maitreyī, Janaka, Gārgī, Uddālaka), the *neti neti* characterization of *ātman*, the *madhu-vidyā*, the *pañcāgni-vidyā*, and the post-mortem trajectory of the *jīva*. The work is the principal vehicle for the refutation of Bhartṛprapañca. Śaṅkara repeatedly stages the *bhedābheda* reading of Yājñavalkya's enumerations and rebuts it, establishing the strict reading of *neti neti* as terminal apophasis rather than as one wing of a dual-aspect doctrine. The *jñāna-karma-samuccaya* doctrine ascribed to Bhartṛprapañca is rejected on the same grounds as in the Īśa-Bhāṣya. Śaṅkara's analysis of the three *avasthās* in the dialogue with Janaka (Bṛh. 4.3) is the *locus classicus* for the Advaita treatment of dream and deep-sleep epistemology. Sureśvara's massive *Bṛhadāraṇyaka-Vārttika* takes this *bhāṣya* as its base. Securely authored.

engaged_works.title: Taittiriya-Bhashya
engaged_works.summary: Commentary on the Taittirīya-Upaniṣad, in three *vallīs*. *Śīkṣā-Vallī* treats phonetics, the introductory *anuvākas*, and the lower *vidyās*. *Brahmānanda-Vallī* — the *Ānanda-Mīmāṃsā* — gives the *pañca-kośa* analysis of *ātman* through five sheaths (*anna-maya*, *prāṇa-maya*, *mano-maya*, *vijñāna-maya*, *ānanda-maya*) and the *brahman*-defining text *satyaṃ jñānam anantaṃ brahma* (Taittirīya 2.1.1). *Bhṛgu-Vallī* gives the dialogue between Varuṇa and Bhṛgu, repeating the *kośa*-progression as a meditative ascent. Śaṅkara's analysis of the *kośa* hierarchy is doctrinally pivotal. He treats *ānanda-maya-kośa* as still belonging to the order of *upādhi*, not as *brahman* itself — a reading explicitly opposed by Rāmānuja, who identifies *ānanda-maya* with the supreme. The exegesis of *satyaṃ jñānam anantaṃ brahma* is the standard Advaita *locus* for the doctrine that *brahman*'s positive characterizations (*sat*, *cit*, *ānanda*) are *svarūpa-lakṣaṇa*, not *guṇas* in the Vaiśeṣika sense. Sureśvara's *Taittirīya-Vārttika* is the standard sub-commentary.

engaged_works.title: Vivekachudamani
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Embraced by the Advaita living tradition; modern scholarship divided, with several scholars (e.g., Grimes' defense vs. Hacker's skepticism) actively debating authorship.
engaged_works.summary: School-ascribed *prakaraṇa* in approximately 580 verses (the manuscript count varies). The work is structured as the systematic instruction of a qualified seeker through the standard Advaita pedagogical sequence: the *sādhana-catuṣṭaya* (the four prerequisites — *viveka*, *vairāgya*, the *ṣaṭ-sampatti*, *mumukṣutva*); *śravaṇa*–*manana*–*nididhyāsana*; the *pañca-kośa-viveka*; the *avasthā-traya-viveka*; the *dṛk*–*dṛśya-viveka*; the *mahāvākya* analysis; and the culminating doctrine of *jīvanmukti*. The work is the single most influential Advaita primer in the living tradition. Ascription is contested. Paul Hacker's stylometric work argues against Śaṅkara's authorship; John Grimes (2004) defends it on doctrinal and stylistic grounds. The Advaita tradition embraces the work as Śaṅkara's, and its devotional and meditatively-instructive register is part of how Advaita-as-living-tradition presents itself — distinct from the strictly *bhāṣya*-based academic Śaṅkara. The "school-ascribed" status here registers both that embrace and the academic doubt.

engaged_works.title: Aparokshanubhuti
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Embraced by the Advaita living tradition; modern academic ascription divided.
engaged_works.summary: School-ascribed *prakaraṇa* in 144 verses on the direct, immediate (*aparokṣa*) realization of the *ātman*. Structurally compact. The work opens with the *sādhana-catuṣṭaya*, then develops a sequence of *vicāra*-questions through which the seeker disidentifies the *ātman* from body, *prāṇa*, *manas*, *buddhi*, and *ahaṃkāra*. It then sets out a fifteen-limbed (*pañcadaśa-aṅga*) *yoga*, explicitly framed in *aṣṭāṅga*-style language with each limb redefined non-dually: *yama* as restraint of the organs in the recognition of *brahman*-identity, *āsana* as steadiness in *brahman*-cognition, *prāṇāyāma* as the cessation of objectifying mental movement. The heavy use of *yogic* technical vocabulary in an Advaitic key is the same feature that makes the academic ascription set hesitant about Śaṅkara's authorship and that makes the tradition value the work as a practical meditation manual. The Advaita tradition embraces this as Śaṅkara's.

engaged_works.title: Saundarya-Lahari
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Embraced by the Advaita and Śrīvidyā living traditions; academic opinion divided on authorship of part or all of the hymn.
engaged_works.summary: School-ascribed *stotra* in 100 verses (in the standard recension), addressed to the Goddess as *Tripurasundarī* / *Lalitā*, the supreme Śakti of the Śrīvidyā tradition. Traditionally divided into two halves. *Ānanda-Laharī* (verses 1–41) gives the metaphysical-theological exposition of the Goddess as identical with the supreme, including the *śrīcakra* and *kuṇḍalinī* references that fix the work in the Śrīvidyā lineage. *Saundarya-Laharī* proper (verses 42–100) gives the *aṅga-varṇana* — the limb-by-limb iconographic celebration of the Goddess's form. The text is the central liturgical poem of Śrīvidyā and a major Sanskrit *stotra* in its own right. Ascription is contested. Many scholars argue at least the second half is post-Śaṅkara; the tradition embraces the whole as Śaṅkara's. The work's frank embrace of a personal-theistic Śakti as supreme is doctrinally significant: it bears on whether Advaita-as-living-tradition is as gap-distant from theistic Vedānta as the *Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya* alone would suggest.

engaged_works.title: Bhaja-Govindam
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Embraced by the Advaita living tradition; uses Vaiṣṇava devotional idiom that the academic ascription set is wary of attributing to the bhāṣya-author.
engaged_works.summary: Also called *Mohamudgara*, "Hammer for Delusion." A short *stotra* of approximately 31 verses, with sub-recensions that ascribe the additional verses to Śaṅkara's twelve *śiṣyas*. The work is framed as a teaching to a grammar-pedant whom Śaṅkara overhears rote-drilling Pāṇinian rules in old age. The opening refrain — *bhaja govindaṃ bhaja govindaṃ govindaṃ bhaja mūḍha-mate / saṃprāpte sannihite kāle nahi nahi rakṣati ḍukṛñ-karaṇe* — subordinates technical learning to *Govinda*-*bhakti* in the face of mortality. Two features are doctrinally consequential: the *vairāgya* register, and the choice of *Govinda* (a Vaiṣṇava name) as the addressed deity. Together they show the Advaita tradition not merely tolerating but actively cultivating personal-theistic devotional address as a *sādhana*. The Advaita tradition embraces the work as Śaṅkara's. Its devotional idiom is one of the load-bearing pieces of evidence on the question whether Advaita-as-living-tradition is as gap-distant from theistic Vedānta as the *Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya* alone would suggest.

engaged_works.title: Sivananda-Lahari
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Embraced by the Advaita and Śaiva living traditions; academic opinion divided.
engaged_works.summary: School-ascribed *stotra* in 100 verses to Śiva, in highly ornate Sanskrit. The work uses heavy *śleṣa* (double meaning) and elaborate metaphor cycles — the bee-and-lotus, the river-and-ocean — for the *bhakta*'s longing for and absorption into Śiva. Doctrinally the hymn is unambiguously *bhakti*-saturated: the speaker presents himself as *dāsa* of Śiva, repeatedly affirms the personal-theistic relation, and treats *Śivānanda* — the bliss of the personal Lord — as the seeker's *paramārtha*-fruit. Tradition pairs the *Śivānanda-Laharī* with the *Saundarya-Laharī* (Goddess) and the *Bhaja-Govindam* (Viṣṇu / Govinda) as the school's three principal devotional anchors across the three theistic streams. Academic ascription is divided. The Advaita tradition embraces the work as Śaṅkara's, and its frank embrace of *bhakti* as the operative *sādhana*-register bears on the same comparative question raised by the other two *stotras*: how distant Advaita-as-living-tradition is from Viśiṣṭādvaita is not settled by the *Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya* alone.

key_passages.sanskrit_iast:
na tato 'mṛtatāśāsti /
karmaṇo 'jñānahetutaḥ /
mokṣasya jñānahetutvān /
na tadanyad apekṣate //

pada_analysis.gloss: because it has ignorance as its cause
because of having knowledge as its cause

karaka_structure.note: Subject of asti.
Ablative source-domain: 'from action'.
Ablative of reason explaining why karma cannot yield immortality.
Implicit subject understood from mokṣasya in the second hemistich.
Object not depended upon.
Ablative of reason: mokṣa has jñāna as its causal condition.

key_passages.english_close: From that, no hope of immortality exists. For action has ignorance as its cause. Mokṣa, by contrast, has knowledge as its cause. Therefore it does not depend on anything else.

preserved_terms.first_use_gloss: (roughly: action, especially prescribed ritual or duty)

key_passages.why_this_passage: This is not a stray maxim but a causal thesis in miniature: Śaṅkara explicitly ties karma to the ignorance-governed order and mokṣa to jñāna alone. It fairly represents his position because the denial of jñāna-karma-samuccaya is a standing commitment across his secure corpus, and here it is stated without dependence on later sub-school terminology.
key_passages.source_edition: GRETIL plaintext of Upadeśasāhasrī based on Sengaku Mayeda, Śaṅkara's Upadeśasāhasrī, vol. 1 (Delhi: Motilal Banarsidass, 2006; 1st ed. Tokyo, 1973), materials/primary_texts/sanskrit/vedanta/full_corpus/shankara_upadesa_sahasri_gretil.txt.

key_passages.sanskrit_iast:
anyadṛṣṭis tv avidyā syāt /
tannāśo mokṣa ucyate /
jñānenaiva tu so 'pi syād /
virodhitvān na karmaṇā //

karmakāryas tv anityaḥ syād /
avidyākāmakāraṇaḥ /
pramāṇaṃ veda evātra /
jñānasyādhigame smṛtaḥ //

jñānaikārthaparatvāt taṃ /
vākyam ekaṃ tato viduḥ /
ekatvaṃ hy ātmano jñeyaṃ /
vākyārthapratipattitaḥ //

pada_analysis.morphology: particle (surface form of tu before vowel)
3sg opt P; surface sandhi form of syāt
particle (surface form of tu before vowel)
3sg opt P; surface sandhi form of syāt

pada_analysis.gloss: having ignorance and desire as its causes

pada_analysis.morphology: particle; split from surface token evātra with following atra
indecl; split from surface token evātra with preceding eva

pada_analysis.gloss: because of being directed to one purport, namely knowledge

pada_analysis.morphology: particle (surface form of hi before vowel)

samasa_vigrahas.resolution: avidyā ca kāmaś ca kāraṇe yasya
jñānam eva eka arthaḥ; tasmin paratvaṃ yasya
samasa_vigrahas.note: A compressed abstract compound: 'because it is oriented to the single purport of knowledge'.

karaka_structure.note: Subject of syāt.
Predicate-nominative in passive with ucyate: 'is called mokṣa'.
What is called mokṣa in the passive construction.
Instrument by which mokṣa comes about.
Excluded instrument: not by karma.
Ablative of reason: karma is opposed.
Subject of syāt in the second verse.
Subject of the passive predicate smṛtaḥ.
Genitive relation with adhigame.
Domain in which the Veda functions as pramāṇa.
Object of viduḥ.
Ablative source: from grasping the sentence-meaning.

verb_modality.note: Occurs three times in the passage.

key_passages.english_close: The seeing of something other [than the self] would be avidyā. The destruction of that is what is called mokṣa. And that [destruction] too would come about only through jñāna. For karma, being its [logical] opponent, cannot accomplish it. What is produced by karma would be impermanent. Such a karma-effect has avidyā and desire as its causes. Here the Veda alone is held to be the pramāṇa. It is remembered as such with respect to the attainment of jñāna. Because [the Veda] is directed to a single purport — namely jñāna. They therefore know that mahā-vākya to be a single unified statement. For the oneness of the self is to be known. And it is known through the comprehension of the sentence-meaning.

preserved_terms.first_use_gloss: (roughly: action, especially prescribed ritual or duty)

key_passages.why_this_passage: This passage is unusually useful because Śaṅkara connects four load-bearing claims in one place: difference-seeing is avidyā, mokṣa is its destruction, karma yields only the impermanent, and Vedic sentence-meaning is the pramāṇa for non-dual knowledge. That combination makes it a fair representation of his epistemology and not a cherry-picked slogan.
key_passages.source_edition: GRETIL plaintext of Upadeśasāhasrī based on Sengaku Mayeda, Śaṅkara's Upadeśasāhasrī, vol. 1 (Delhi: Motilal Banarsidass, 2006; 1st ed. Tokyo, 1973), materials/primary_texts/sanskrit/vedanta/full_corpus/shankara_upadesa_sahasri_gretil.txt.

key_passages.locus_long: Māṇḍūkya-Upaniṣad 7, as glossed in Śaṅkara's bhāṣya
key_passages.sanskrit_iast:
nāntaḥprajñaṃ na bahiḥprajñaṃ nobhayataḥprajñaṃ na prajñānaghanam /
na prajñaṃ nāprajñam /
adṛṣṭam avyavahāryam agrāhyam alakṣaṇam acintyam avyapadeśyam /
ekātmapratyayasāraṃ prapañcopaśamaṃ śāntaṃ śivam advaitaṃ caturthaṃ manyante /
sa ātmā sa vijñeyaḥ //

pada_analysis.gloss: whose essence is the conviction of one self

samasa_vigrahas.resolution: eka ātmā iti pratyayaḥ sāraṃ yasya
samasa_vigrahas.note: Śaṅkara's bhāṣya explicitly glosses this around the unwavering cognition 'eko 'yam ātmā'.

karaka_structure.note: Object-complement of manyante: the described reality is considered 'the fourth'.
Implicit subject of manyante.
Subject of the final nominal sentence.

key_passages.english_close: Not inwardly cognizing, not outwardly cognizing, not cognizing both ways, not a mass of cognition; not cognizing, not non-cognizing; unseen, not available for transaction, not graspable, without defining mark, unthinkable, not nameable; whose essence is the conviction of one self, the cessation of phenomenal proliferation, peaceful, auspicious, non-dual, they consider [that] the fourth. That is the self; that is to be known.
key_passages.why_this_passage: This mantra is famous because Śaṅkara makes it the apex-definition of the whole Māṇḍūkya teaching, not because later anthologies happened to favor it. In the supplied corpus it is the clearest compact locus for his strategy of denying every state-bound predicate while preserving the self as non-dual consciousness rather than collapsing it into non-being.
key_passages.source_edition: Existing local text / GRETIL plaintext, Māṇḍūkya-Upaniṣad with Māṇḍūkyamūlamantrabhāṣya, Gauḍapāda-Kārikā, and Śaṅkara bhāṣya, materials/primary_texts/sanskrit/vedanta/full_corpus/mandukya_upanisad_gaudapada_shankara_bhasya_existing.txt; header notes the mūla checked against V. P. Limaye and R. D. Vadekar (Poona, 1958) and the Kārikā text against A. V. Kathavate (Poona, 1890).

key_passages.locus_long: Gauḍapāda-Kārikā 2.32, as glossed in Śaṅkara's bhāṣya
key_passages.sanskrit_iast:
na nirodho na cotpattir /
na baddho na ca sādhakaḥ /
na mumukṣur na vai mukta /
ity eṣā paramārthatā //

pada_analysis.morphology: particle; split from surface token cotpattir with following utpattiḥ
nom sg fem; split from surface token cotpattir with preceding ca

key_passages.english_close: There is no cessation and no origination. There is no one bound and no practitioner. There is no seeker of liberation, and indeed no liberated one. This is the highest truth.
key_passages.why_this_passage: This verse is worth selecting because Śaṅkara's own gloss at the same locus unpacks each negation as paramārthika rather than merely rhetorical. It therefore fairly represents his ajāti reading of causation and his insistence that bondage, practice, and release belong to the empirical teaching context rather than to Brahman's ultimate status.
key_passages.source_edition: Existing local text / GRETIL plaintext, Māṇḍūkya-Upaniṣad with Māṇḍūkyamūlamantrabhāṣya, Gauḍapāda-Kārikā, and Śaṅkara bhāṣya, materials/primary_texts/sanskrit/vedanta/full_corpus/mandukya_upanisad_gaudapada_shankara_bhasya_existing.txt; header notes the mūla checked against V. P. Limaye and R. D. Vadekar (Poona, 1958) and the Kārikā text against A. V. Kathavate (Poona, 1890).
